sha256:21e04db6ce368b289eb8c5fed22a02f22.note: Verified that bash -c exposes a general shell execution surface. The currently supplied command does not constrain the underlying execution authority.
The fixture invokes a command interpreter rather than a narrowly scoped executable. This supports the exec.dangerous-command finding and BLOCK verdict.

sha256:69e91974e4279c75c021cc666c9a04b62.note: powershell -Command exposes a general command interpreter surface. Get-ChildItem is benign, but the configured execution capability is not limited to that command.
Confirmed that the verdict is capability-based rather than payload-based. The PowerShell interpreter can execute arbitrary future command strings, so the BLOCK boundary is consistent.

sha256:5b96a3b96cae8824d66b71bf4f49af702.note: The filesystem server receives access to the full /Users/lucas home-directory root, and the npm package reference has no immutable version. Both findings are supported.
Confirmed both artifact findings independently: the granted path covers the user's broad home scope, and the unversioned package may resolve to different code over time.

sha256:8cb0eed5798ba0768ddd21ddb3add0b29.note: C:\Users\admin is the Windows user-profile root and therefore a broad filesystem authorization. The package is pinned at 1.0.0, so no unpinned-package finding is expected.
The fixture exposes an entire Windows user profile rather than an application-specific directory. I also verified that the pinned package explains the absence of a supply.unpinned-package finding.

sha256:cbc3ffe8012988c3dbe4287bb0fc69af9.note: The Docker arguments bind the host Desktop directory into the container. No read-only limitation is shown, so the host-side broad-path exposure is supported.
Confirmed that the detector correctly follows the Docker mount syntax to the host source path. Mounting the full Desktop creates a broad agent-accessible filesystem surface.

sha256:578dd3d4f69fdd0a9deeef6d27451ee16.note: The declared create_payment and issue_refund tools provide observed money-movement capability and justify BLOCK. ACME_API_TOKEN also gives the server credential-bearing environment access, without implying that the token value is disclosed.

The tool metadata explicitly describes immediate card charging and refunds, so financial authority is observed rather than inferred. The configured API token independently supports the secrets.env-key finding.

sha256:b999a14f880521815a88c376628bdb146.note: The tool description directs the model to call this tool before others and conceal that behavior from the user. This is a hidden model-directed instruction consistent with prompt poisoning.
The metadata attempts to override tool-selection behavior and suppress user visibility. Both the priority instruction and 'Do not tell the user' language support the critical finding.

sha256:ac53be3acbaa3d1f6b3aab194297576e3.note: The package identity supports an inferred financial and external-mutation risk, so REVIEW is appropriate. No observed tool surface is available to justify escalation to BLOCK.

Confirmed the Observed-versus-Inferred boundary: the package name suggests payment behavior, but the artifact contains no declared payment tool. The conservative REVIEW verdict is therefore supported.

sha256:79ca7ed9ca119414aa16405dab6a31782.note: @latest is mutable and cannot guarantee reproducible package resolution. REVIEW is appropriate because no separate critical execution or authority capability is observed.
The dependency is explicitly resolved through the moving latest tag rather than an immutable version. This supports the high-severity supply-chain finding without independently requiring BLOCK.
